coredata.dc:title: Green route for synthesis of multifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from Tulsi leaves and its application as Cr(VI) sensors, bio-imaging and patterning agents
coredata.dc:creator: Bhatt, Shreya
Bhatt, Madhuri
Kumar, Anshu
Vyas, Gaurav
Gajaria, Tejal
Paul, Parimal
coredata.dc:description: We report a one pot green strategy for the synthesis of carbon dots using tulsi leaves and their potential application in sensing of Cr(VI) selectively. The detection mechanism is based on the phenomenon called inner filter effect (IFE) and a good linear static quenching was observed in the range of 1.6 μM to 50 μM with a detection limit of 4.5 ppb. The reversible switching in fluorescence has been tested and a good recovery in fluorescence was observed up to three consecutive cycles upon addition of ascorbic acid as reducing agent. Also the low toxicity, high fluorescence and photostabilty of the CDs make them excellent imaging and patterning agent. The acid and alkali resistant property of these CDs makes it suitable for real sample analysis. The fluorescent CDs were applied for successful detection of Cr(VI) in water with spike-recoveries ranging from 93 to 99%.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Inner filter effect
Chromium (VI)
Imaging
Patterning
Real sample analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Sensing mechanism of CDs for Cr(VI)

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Council of Scientific and Industrial Research, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: CSIR-CSMCRI publication number: 173/2017. Financial support from CSIR and fellowship from University Grant Commission (AK) are gratefully acknowledged. We thank G. R. Bhadu, Dr. D. N. Srivastava, J. C. Chaudhary, Laiya Riddhi P. and V. Vakani for recording TEM, SEM images, XRD data and IR spectra, respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of hydrothermal synthesis of CDs from Tulsi leaves and its behaviour under normal day light and UV-light.
(A, B) TEM images and (C, D and E) elemental mapping of carbon dots. Red = carbon, green = nitrogen, blue = oxygen.
(A) FTIR spectra of CDs and (B) Powder XRD of C-Dots.

(A) Fluorescence spectra of the CDs in presence of 11 different anions, (B) Fluorescence spectra of CDs in presence of different concentration of Cr(VI) at different time intervals (C) Bar diagram showing interference of different anions with Cr(VI), (D) Titration spectra of CDs with increasing concentration of Cr(VI), (E) The plot of F0/F against Cr(VI) concentration and (F) Decay curve of CDs only (blue) and in presence of Cr(VI) (pink). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

(A) Overlay of absorption spectra of Cr(VI) with excitation and emission spectra of CDs. (B) UV–vis spectral data of CDs and with Cr(VI). (C) Zeta potential of only CDs and in presence of different concentrations of Cr(VI) (D) Schematic presentation showing sensing mechanism in presence of Cr(VI).

(A) Reversibility of CDs in presence of ascorbic acid (B) Linear calibration plotted by different known concentration of Cr(V) with corresponding values of Cr(VI) in real sample, drinking water (blue), Tap water(red) and industrial waste (green). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

Bio imaging of bacteria in presence of CDs in blue, yellow and red region (A) E-Coli and (B) B.Subtilis. (C) Patterning images drawn with carbon nanoink under UV light. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.).

head.ce:title: Green route for synthesis of multifunctional fluorescent carbon dots from Tulsi leaves and its application as Cr(VI) sensors, bio-imaging and patterning agents

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Academy of Scientific and Innovative Research (AcSIR)

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Division of Biotechnology and Phycology, CSIR-Central Salt & Marine Chemicals Research Institute (CSMCRI), Bhavnagar, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CSIR-Central Salt & Marine Chemicals Research Institute

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility, CSIR-CSMCRI, Bhavnagar, India.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Analytical and Environmental Science Division and Centralized Instrument Facility

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We report a one pot green strategy for the synthesis of carbon dots using tulsi leaves and their potential application in sensing of Cr(VI) selectively. The detection mechanism is based on the phenomenon called inner filter effect (IFE) and a good linear static quenching was observed in the range of 1.6 μM to 50 μM with a detection limit of 4.5 ppb. The reversible switching in fluorescence has been tested and a good recovery in fluorescence was observed up to three consecutive cycles upon addition of ascorbic acid as reducing agent. Also the low toxicity, high fluorescence and photostabilty of the CDs make them excellent imaging and patterning agent. The acid and alkali resistant property of these CDs makes it suitable for real sample analysis. The fluorescent CDs were applied for successful detection of Cr(VI) in water with spike-recoveries ranging from 93 to 99%.

ce:section.ce:para: Detection of metal ions and anions of interest with high selectivity and sensitivity, particularly in aqueous media, is an important area of contemporary research because of their potential applications in environmental, industrial and biological sciences [1,2]. Among various transition metals, Cr(VI) is one of the most toxic ions and exposure to Cr(VI) leads to cancer and it damages the gastrointestinal, respiratory and immunological systems [3]. As per the norms of EPA, the Maximum Contaminant Level Goals for total chromium concentrations in drinking water should be under 100 ppb [4]. However, the highly soluble character of Cr(VI) and its enormous discharge from various electroplating, tanning and leather industries has resulted into a considerable increase in its concentration in drinking water [5]. Therefore, development of analytical methods for detection of trace amount of Cr(VI) in environment is of crucial importance.

A variety of analytical techniques including chromatography, electrochemical techniques, atomic absorption spectrometry and many more have been developed for the detection of Cr(VI) [6,7]. However, most of these techniques require costly instruments, lengthily sample preparation procedure, large quantity of sample and highly skilled technical person. Some of these methods are also not suitable for detection of trace amount of Cr(VI). As alternative, the non-destructive colorimetric and fluorometric detection methods are most attractive in terms of selectivity, sensitivity, response time and easiness in application and cost effectiveness [8–10]. Among various fluorescent sensors for metal ions, carbon dots (CDs) have emerged as most attractive because of its excellent properties such as good biocompatibility, robustness, resistance to photobleaching, low toxicity, cost effectiveness etc [11–14]. Considering these advantages, we aimed to develop a simple method for preparation of carbon dots for its possible application to detect highly toxic and carcinogenic metal ions such as Cr(VI).

Herein we report a one pot green strategy for the synthesis of water dispersible fluorescent CDs using Tulsi leaves as carbon precursor, which selectively detect Cr(VI) in aqueous media. There are examples of CD based sensors for Cr(VI), however those CDs were prepared from various commercially available compounds as carbon sources [12–16]. As nature precursors for CDs for sensing of Cr(VI), use of wool and pig hair is reported [17]. However, to the best of our knowledge, no plant leaves derived CDs has been reported for sensing of Cr(VI). Tulsi leaves are readily available, inexpensive (has hardly any commercial value) and can be converted to CDs by an easy one-step green process. Detail investigation revealed that the quenching in fluorescence intensity upon addition of Cr(VI) is due to Inner filter effect (IFE). The fluorescence of the CDs is recovered by using ascorbic acid as reducing agent, which converts Cr(VI) to Cr(III) resulting in regain of fluorescence intensity, which allows the system to be re-used [18]. To demonstrate the practical utility of these CDs, it was used for the detection of Cr(VI) in real samples such as industrial waste water and the results obtained are satisfactory. Due to good biocompatibility and photostability, these CDs are used as excellent bio-imaging and patterning agents.

Fresh leaves of Tulsi were collected from the garden of this institute (CSIR-CSMCRI) and washed thoroughly with Milli-Q water before use. Tetrabutylammonium (TBA+) salts of fluoride, bromide, chloride, iodide, periodate, cyanide, dihydrogen phosphate, acetate, nitrate, bisulfate, Poly vinyl Alcohol (MW 89,000–98,000) 99% Hydrolysed and Quinine sulphate were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Potassium Dichromate was purchased from Merck Ltd, India. Ethanol was purchased from SD-Fine Chemicals Ltd (SDFCL), India. Solutions of all these chemicals were prepared using Milli-Q water.

The CDs were prepared using hydrothermal process, in a typical procedure 2.5 gms of Tulsi leaves were finely chopped into small pieces and 35 mL of Milli-Q water was added to it. The mixture was then transferred to a 50 mL vessel followed by hydrothermal treatment at 200 °C for 4 h. After completion of the reaction, the vessels were allowed to cool down at room temperature and the aqueous suspensions were filtered using 0.22 μm filter membrane in order to remove larger particles. The yellowish dispersed fine particles were obtained and the final volume was adjusted to 100 mL with the effective concentration of 5 mg mL−1 and stored at 4 °C for further studies. Storage at low temperature is to avoid microbial contamination, as the CDs are non-toxic in nature (discussed in the later section).

The CDs were characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM) upon drop casting the appropriately diluted sample onto the copper grids and imaging was conducted on JEOL, model JEM 2100 transmission electron microscope (200 Kv). Elemental mapping was performed with EDX (INCA, Japan) field emission scanning electron microscope (FE-SEM). Wide angle X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) patters were recorded in the range of 0° to 70° on PanAnalytical EMPYREAN (Cu-Kα radiation) with the scan rate of 1°/min. The infrared spectra were recorded on the Perkin-Elmer spectra GX 2000 FT-IR spectrometer using KBr pellets. Elemental analysis was performed using Elementa Vario MICRO CUBE analyzer. Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was performed by Netzch STA 409PC TG-DTA instrument from 50 to 1000 °C with a scanning rate 10 °C min−1 in presence of nitrogen flow. The zeta potential was carried out with the help of Malvern instrument (Zetasizer, Nano series, Nano-ZS90). The fluorescence spectral measurements were recorded on Edinburgh Xe900 spectrophotometer while the absorption spectra were recorded on CARY 500 scan Varian UV–vis spectrophotometer. Time resolved fluorescence decay curves were recorded on Edinburgh OB920 spectroflurometer with the LED source of 365 nm. The microplate reader (spectramax plus 384) was used to measure absorbance at 600 nm. The Bacterial cell imaging was performed using optical microscope (Olympus DP72 U-TVO 63XC).

Quantum yield measurement was performed following the published procedure [19]. Quinine sulphate (0.1 M H2SO4 as solvent, Φ = 0.54) was chosen as a standard to measure the quantum yield (Φ) of the CDs as per other reports [19]. The quantum yield was determined at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm. The absorbance of standard and carbon dots solution was kept below 0.1 at excitation wavelength in order to minimize the reabsorption effects and the Quantum yield was calculated using the following formulae.
(1) Q x = std × I X/I std × std/A x × ƞ2 /ƞ2 std
Where Q std is the quantum yield of the standard compound, I is the integrated emission intensity, A is the absorbance recorded at excitation wavelength while ƞ is the refractive index of the solvent used. “x” refers to the sample whose quantum yield is to be determined while “std” refers to the standard used (Quinine sulphate).

All the anion solutions were prepared from their respective Tetra butylammonium salts. Potassium dichromate was used as chromate source, and all the solutions were prepared with the concentration of 2 mM in Milli-Q water. Detection of anions was carried out purely in aqueous media. Typically, 1 mL of 2 mM of each of the anion solution was added to 400 μL of the CDs (2.0 mg mL−1) and the final volume was adjusted to 3 mL. For the detection of Cr(VI), various concentration to 2 mM solution of chromate were added to 400 μL of carbon Dots solution and the fluorescence spectra was recorded at 365 nm.

In order to check reversibility and reusability of the system ascorbic acid was chosen as reducing agent to carry out the reduction of Cr(VI) in aqueous media. Ascorbic acid has proved to be an efficient mild reducing agent and is also known to act over a wide pH range [18]. For this purpose, 6 mM ascorbic acid solution was prepared as stock. Initially Cr(VI) (200 uL, 2 mM) was added to the solution containing carbon dots (200 uL, 1 mg mL−1) and the change in the spectra was recorded. This was followed by addition of ascorbic acid (200 uL, 6 mM) and the change in fluorescence intensity was recorded. The cycle was repeated three to four times until significant recovery in fluorescence intensity was observed and the spectra was recorded.

The Broth Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC) test was performed in order to check the toxic effect of the carbon dots over the bacterial strains. The bacterial cell cultures of Gram-negative Escherichia coli and Gram-positive Bacillus subtilis were used for MIC testing. The bacterial cultures were revived from the glycerol stocks by adding 100 μL of stock to the 50 mL freshly prepared sterilized Luria-Bertani broth followed by incubation at 37 °C overnight at 120 rpm. The cells were incubated with different concentrations of carbon dots for 12 h and the bacterial growth in the medium was quantified by taking the absorbance at 600 nm.

Fluorescence images of bacteria, B. subtilis and E-coli, was carried out by using CDs as a probe. The isolated colony of E-coli and B. subtilis was inoculated in 50 mL sterile LB both containing casein enzymatic hydrolysate-10 gm/litre, yeast extract-5 gm/litre and sodium chloride-10 gm/litre at pH 7.0. The cultures were kept in overnight incubation at 37 °C and 50 μL aliquot was transferred into a fresh nutrient medium (2 mL), stirred and cultured at 37 °C for 6 h. The sample was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min, and the pellet was collected and washed twice in PBS, pH-7. To prepare the bacterial-CDs conjugates, the pellet was incubated with 70% (v/v) ethanol at 4 °C for 5 min followed by addition of 100 μL CDs and incubation of 1 h in absence of light. To remove the unadheared CDs from the medium, the cells were washed twice with PBS; pH-7 and finally dispersed in 100 μL of PBS; pH-7 for fluorescence imaging. The Fluorescence images of the internalized cells were recorded in blue, green and red region upon their excitation at 405, 488 and 561 nm respectively.

In the quest of exploring further applications of CDs, their role as patterning agents was investigated. For this, the carbon nano-ink was prepared by adding 5 mL of 5% PVA solution to 5 mL of the carbon dots solution and the mixture was stirred for 15 min in order to gain viscosity in the ink. The solution thus prepared was poured into the vacant already used ink-pen refill. The so prepared fluorescent carbon nano-ink was further utilized for drawing various patterns and alphabets on a commercially available paper with different backgrounds and the fluorescent images were obtained under UV light.

Tulsi is widely distributed throughout the world and is known for the treatment of several ailments including ozena, skin diseases, gastric and various stress induced disorders [20]. The characteristics of this herb are due to the variety of functional moieties present within it. These functional groups are rich in carbon, nitrogen and oxygen species that can be tailored onto the surface of carbon dots thereby preventing the use of any additional passivating agent. Hence Tulsi leaves act as an excellent precursor for the preparation of CDs. The CDs were thus successfully prepared by one step hydrothermal treatment followed by filtration through 0.22 μm filter membrane, which resulted into a golden yellow solution with dispersed fine particles obtained by successful carbonization of Tulsi leaves (Scheme 1 ).

The morphology of the prepared CDs was determined by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). The Fig. 1 a revealed well dispersed formation of carbon dots with spherical morphology with the average size around 5 nm. The image of individual carbon nanoparticle (Fig. 1b) represents their high crystallinity with the lattice parameter of 0.286 nm, which corresponded to the (020) diffraction plane of graphitic (sp2) carbon [21].

The elemental analysis reveals that the composition of the prepared CDs is C 41.43 wt%, H 4.81 wt%, N 4.83 wt% and O (calculated) 48.92 wt% (Table S1). The high amount of carbon and oxygen content indicates the presence of large number of carbon and oxygen containing functional groups with a few nitrogen containing groups on the surface of these carbonaceous nanomaterials.

The EDS elemental mapping distributions (Fig. 1(c–e)) of the CDs further confirmed the abundant presence of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen species which was found to be in good agreement with the C, H, N and O data. The presence of functional groups was further confirmed by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2 a). The strong peak at 3318 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibration of the OH/NH groups, peaks at 2958, 1288 and 807 cm−1 are due to the CH stretching and bending vibrations. Strong peaks at 1606 and 1415 cm−1 are assigned to CO and CC stretching vibration, respectively, other peaks appeared at 1120 and 1089 cm−1 are due to the stretching and bending vibrations of CO bonds in the carboxyl groups [22,23].

The IR data suggests the presence of various hydroxyl, carboxylic, carbonyl and amino containing functional groups on the surface of CDs. In order to investigate the changes during the hydrothermal treatment the CDs were prepared at 50, 100 and 150 °C and the effect on surface functional group was studied. The IR spectra of these CDs does not exhibit significant change in the peaks positions, however difference in the relative intensities of certain peaks were observed (Fig. S1). This is possibly due to abundance of various functional groups on the surface of the CDs which changes with increasing temperature [24].

The XRD diffraction peaks (Fig. 2b) with d spacing values of 0.281 and 0.318 nm corresponded to the (020) and (002) faces, respectively, of graphite [21,25]. The Raman spectra (Fig. S2) exhibits two broad peaks at approximately 1339 and 1594 cm−1 which are attributed to the D band (sp3 hybridized) and G band (sp2 hybridized). The peak at 1482 cm−1 is attributed to the asymmetric bending of the methyl groups. Also the characteristic peak of covalent CN single bond is seen at 1249 cm−1 in the Raman spectrum [26].

The thermal stability of CDs was investigated by TGA curve as shown in (Fig. S3). The degradation process of the CDs was depicted to be three step as shown in the thermogram. The weight loss started beyond 100 °C with the initial loss of 4.09% which is due to the elimination of water molecules associated with the carbon dots. In the second step the weight loss of 43.8% occurred between 200–550 °C due to the gradual degradation of surface functional groups present on the surface of the CDs. Further increase in temperature above 550 °C till 1000 °C resulted in the mass loss of 28.7% which is due to the combustion of carbon core comprising of various aliphatic and aromatic domains. The zeta potential of the CDs was found to be −25.7 mV, which is attributed to the abundant presence of carboxyl and hydroxyl groups present onto the surface of carbon dots and is found to be in agreement with elemental analysis and the FTIR data.

The CDs were found to have a bluish fluorescence under UV light. The aqueous solution of CDs gives a broad absorption peak at 280 nm, which is attributed to n- π* transition of CO bonds in CDs thereby indicating the broad size distribution of CDs [23–27]. It is evident from the fluorescence spectrum that the CDs showed excitation dependent behaviour with red shift in the emission peak from 410 to 505 nm upon exciting from 300 nm to 440 nm showing the highest emission peak at 435 nm when it is excited at 360 nm (Fig. S4).

The stability of the CDs was examined in acid and alkaline conditions by varying the pH of the medium with 0.1N HCl and 0.1N NaOH solutions and the change in the photoluminescent intensities was recorded (Fig. S5). As evident from the spectra, there was minor change in the photoluminescent intensity of the CDs in the pH range 3–10 which indicates that the CDs are highly stable under this pH range. This property of the CDs facilitates their further application for on-site determination and real sample analysis.

Taking into consideration of the aqueous dispersibility and excellent photoluminescent intensity the CDs were further applied for ion recognition study. The photoluminescence intensity of the CDs in presence of 11 different anions with the concentration of 0.66 mM was studied at neutral pH (Fig. 3 a). As shown in the figure among all other anions tested, Cr2O7 2− i.e Cr(VI) showed substantial quenching in the fluorescence intensity. A minor change in fluorescence was observed in case of iodate (IO4 −), which is possibly due to few non-specific interaction taking place between the anion and the functional groups of the CDs. However, the strong fluorescence quenching observed in case of Cr(VI) revealed that the carbon dots showed higher selectivity towards Cr(VI) among all other anions.

In order to optimize the system for the sensing of chromium, the change in the fluorescence intensities of the CDs at various concentration of Cr(VI) [10 ppb, 100 ppb and 1000 ppb] with time was observed (Fig. 3b). The spectra revealed that no considerable change in the fluorescence intensities could be observed with time at different concentrations of Cr (VI) representing the photostabe nature of the CDs. Therefore, no incubation time was required for the system as the fluorescent intensity remained same at any point of time at different concentrations of Cr(VI). The interference study was carried out with 10 times enhanced concentration of other ions in comparison to Cr(VI) in order to monitor the effect of other interfering ions in presence of Cr(VI) (Fig. 3c). The figure revealed that no other anion showed noticeable interference in presence of Cr(VI), making the system more suitable for Cr(VI) detection.

The fluorescence quenching behaviour of the CDs was studied by Stern-Volmer plot. The change in the fluorescence spectra of the CDs with different concentrations of Cr(VI) ranging from 0 μM to 1.73 mM was recorded and a linear decrease in the fluorescence intensity is observed with increasing concentration of Cr(VI) (Fig. 3d).

The ratio of the fluorescence intensity of the solution before addition of Cr(VI) to that after addition of the chromate anion (F0/F) is plotted as a function of the concentration of Cr(VI) giving rise to a non-linear exponential decay curve. In order to fit the non-linear decay, curve the below stated exponential equation was applied,
(2) Y = Y0 + A exp (R0[X])
Here, Y0, A and R0 are constants. The results indicate that the plot could be best fitted to F0/F = 0.19197 + 1.0632 exp 0.00291* [Cr(VI)] perfectly, with linear correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.997. The upward curvatures are attributed to higher extent of quenching of the CDs in presense of Cr(VI) [28]. The quenching constant (Ksv) was calculated to be 3.16 × 103 mol−1 as shown in Fig. 3e. The CDs depict a perfect linear behaviour in the range of 1.6–50 μM and the limit of detection for Cr(VI) is found to be 4.5 ppb (1.6 μM), which is much lower than the permissible limits set by the EPA [4]. Comparison with the other reported methods for Cr(VI) detection is shown in Table S2 [29–32]. The fluorescence decay curve analysis was also done in terms of lifetime measurement (Fig. 3f) and it revealed that no noticeable change in the average lifetime (4.560 ns) of the CDs and (4.455 ns) CDs + Cr(VI) was observed.

The quantum yield of the carbon dots was determined to be 3.06% (Table S3), which is close to fewer systems prepared from materials of natural origin but is significantly low compared to the CDs obtained from various non-plant sources [23–33]. The possible reason behind the excitation dependant emission property of the carbon dots is the presence of different particle sizes of the carbon dots as well as distribution of different emissive sites on the surface of the carbon dots [34]. The energy gap decreases gradually upon increase in size of the carbon dots due to quantum confinement effect. Hence a mixture of carbon dots with various sizes possess different excitation and emission spectra. Also the presence of variety of functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots results in a series of emissive traps on its surface. Upon imparting a particular excitation energy, the corresponding emissive traps become dominant thereby resulting in different emission spectra. However, the presence of different sizes of carbon dots is responsible for its low quantum yield. This is due to energy transfer process taking place from small size carbon dots to large size carbon dots resulting in luminescent quenching and decreasing its quantum yield [35]. Another possible reason for reduction in quantum yield is due to graphitization which takes place at higher temperature and longer reaction time [36].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanism of CDs for Cr(VI)
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the analysis of all these data the mechanism of detection was studied. As shown in Fig. 4 a, the absorption spectra of the Cr(VI) observed at 257 nm and 350 nm, efficiently overlaps the excitation as well as emission spectra of the carbon dots. This condition matches exactly with the criteria of Inner Filter Effect (IFE), where there is a good spectral overlap between the absorption band of the absorber and Emission and/or excitation band of the same fluorophore. As shown in Fig. 4b the maximum absorption is observed with an obvious red shift in case of CDs + Cr(VI) in comparison to the absorption of only carbon dots and Cr(VI). The result confirmed the absorption of the emitted light by Cr(VI). The histogram (Fig. 4c) revealed that the zeta potential of only CDs was −25.7 mV which is found to be due to the abundant presence of negatively charged groups over the surface of the CDs. However, the zeta potential diminishes gradually upon addition of Cr(VI) which is attributed to the interactions taking place between the Cr(VI) and surface functional groups of the carbon dots. The results of fluorescence decay as shown in Fig. 3f were studied in order to further explore the mechanism and the exponential fits were summarized as shown in Table-S4. The analysis revealed that carbon dots decay curves consisted of two lifetime components and no noticeable change was observed in the average lifetime of the CDs (4.560 ns) and CDs + Cr(VI) (4.455 ns) system which is similar to the conditions of static quenching mechanism. Hence the overall mechanism is found to be a combination of inner filter effect with static quenching which results into efficient turn-off fluorescence of the carbon dots in presence of Cr(VI) (Fig. 4d).

The Fig. 5 a shows the reversible switching of fluorescence upon alternate addition of Cr(VI) and ascorbic acid to the CDs. As evident from the Figure, addition of ascorbic acid resulted in reduction of Cr(VI) to Cr(III) thereby elimination the inner filter effect and the regaining the fluorescence of the carbon dots. The spectra recorded demonstrate a good recovery in the fluorescence intensity in three consecutive cycles. Hence the system could be re-used at least three times for onsite detection of Cr(VI).

To check the usability of the CDs sensing of Cr(VI) in real samples was investigated. As real samples, we include tap and drinking water from the institute and industrial waste collected from leather industries in Chennai (India). Drinking and tap water did not contain Cr(VI) and therefore known amount was spiked in it and water from industrial waste was used as received. The linear range obtained from the titration of Cr(VI) with different concentration, was used to obtain unknown concentration in the real samples as shown in Fig. 5b. Results obtained are satisfactory and shown in Table S5.

Fig. 6 (A and B) shows the imaging of bacterial cells of B. subtilis and E-coli using carbon-dots as the bio-friendly fluorescent probe. The internalized cells were excited at 405, 488 and 561 nm which resulted into the emission of blue, yellow and red fluorescence respectively by the cells. The well dispersed fluorescent images of the bacterial cells indicate the successful penetration of the carbon dots within the cell membrane. The toxicity study towards the bacteria was performed by the MIC broth dilution method and the results demonstrated that no inhibition of bacterial growth was found at various concentrations of the carbon dots (Fig.S6). Also the reaction time needed for the carbon dots to adhere to the bacterial cells in order to form the bacterial-CDs conjugates is quite low which is indicative of the ease with which the carbon dots can be quickly penetrated into the bacterial cells. Hence carbon dots serve as better alternate for cell-imaging in comparison to conventional dyes and quantum dots.

Till date a variety of dyes, polymer composites and carbon nanotubes has been utilized as patterning agents. However, the use of carbon dots made from natural precursors as patterning agents is still rare to best of our knowledge. For this we utilized the so prepared carbon dots for patterning purpose and a variety of hand-made patterns were drawn on the commercially available paper and then air dried. As shown in Fig. 6C, highly fluorescent patterns were visible under the UV light making carbon dots an efficient patterning agents. The printed patterns were stable till 3 months which could be beneficial for their practical applications. Such patterns can be used for conveying highly confidential messages to maintain the security standards and information storage.

In summary we report a simple one step green process for preparation of carbon dots from Tulsi leaves, which selectively detect Cr(VI) in aqueous media. The detection mechanism is based on IFE. The acid and alkali resistant property of the carbon dot makes them more suitable for on-site application of real samples. The CDs thus prepared could recycled upon addition of ascorbic acid. The photostability, low toxicity and good photoluminescence intensity of the carbon dots make them potential imaging agents for imaging of bacterial cells and excellent patterning agent.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: CSIR-CSMCRI publication number: 173/2017. Financial support from CSIR and fellowship from University Grant Commission (AK) are gratefully acknowledged. We thank G. R. Bhadu, Dr. D. N. Srivastava, J. C. Chaudhary, Laiya Riddhi P. and V. Vakani for recording TEM, SEM images, XRD data and IR spectra, respectively.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.colsurfb.2018.04.008.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nanostructured sensors for detection of heavy metals: a review
Carbon quantum dot-based nanoprobes for metal ion detection
Effects of chromium on the immune system
Selective adsorption of chromium (VI) in industrial wastewater using low-cost abundantly available adsorbents
A review on detection of heavy metal ions In water – an electrochemical approach
Micelle-mediated methodology for speciation of chromium by flame atomic absorption spectrometry
Revisiting the use of Gold and Silver Functionalised Nanoparticles as Colorimetric and Fluorometric Chemosensors for metal ions
Fluorescent and colorimetric electrospun nanofibers for heavy-metal sensing
Review of the application of quantum dots in the heavy-metal detection
Plant leaf-derived fluorescent carbon dots for sensing, patterning and coding
An efficient “off–on” carbon nanoparticle-based fluorescent sensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Facile one-step synthesis of highly luminescent N-doped carbon dots as an efficient fluorescent probe for chromium(VI) detection based on the inner filter effect
On-Off-On fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Fluorescent carbon dots for the sensitive detection of Cr(VI) in aqueous media and their application in test papers
High luminescent carbon dots as an eco-friendly fluorescence sensor for Cr(VI) determination in water and soil samples
One-step fabrication of high quantum yield sulfurand nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective detection of Cr(VI)
Reduction of hexavalent chromium by ascorbic acid in aqueous solutions
